title: Lesson 12 on Eschatology: The Resurrection of the Dead
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 12 on Eschatology_ The Resurrection of the Dead.txt

outline.title: Review: Revelation 19-20 and the Structure of John's Vision
subpoints: Revelation 19:11-19 depicts Christ's physical return as a warrior conquering on a white horse
If chapter 19 is not Christ's physical return, the book of Revelation does not address it at all
Chapters 20 and 21 function as a commentary and elaboration of chapter 19's vision
The structure is parallel (like Genesis 1 and 2), not strictly sequential
The recurring pattern of the seven seals, trumpets, and bowls shows Revelation's concurrent structure

outline.title: The Crux: Revelation 20:4-5 and the Meaning of 'Came to Life'
subpoints: Two uses of 'came to life' appear only 13 words apart (in the original Greek)
The ESV translation 'they came to life' is more accurate than the NKJV 'they lived'
The grammatical form is third person plural active indicative — past tense describing what John saw
The central question: is this first 'came to life' a spiritual or physical resurrection?
No view — including the pastor's own — fully resolves every difficulty in these verses
Amillennial and postmillennial positions take the first 'came to life' as spiritual (regeneration); the second as physical

outline.title: Exegesis: 'Came to Life' Means Physical Resurrection throughout Revelation
subpoints: Ephesians 2:1-6 uses a related Greek word to describe spiritual resurrection at salvation — but it is a different word in a different context
John 5:25: 'the hour is coming and now is when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God' — this is spiritual resurrection in context
Revelation 2:8: Christ is described as he 'who died and came to life' — clearly physical resurrection
Revelation 13:14: the beast 'who was wounded by the sword and lived' — a literal coming back to life
Revelation 20:5: all positions agree this second 'came to life' is a physical resurrection
Consistency: in all 42 New Testament uses, the word 'resurrection' (anastasis) refers to a physical bodily resurrection — Revelation 20:4 would be the sole exception if interpreted spiritually

outline.title: Two Resurrections in Other Scriptures
subpoints: John 5:28-29: 'Do not marvel at this, for the hour is coming in which all who are in the graves will hear His voice and come forth — those who have done good, to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil, to the resurrection of condemnation'
Jesus separates the two resurrections by repeating the phrase 'the resurrection of' — unnecessary unless they are distinct
The word 'hour' frequently denotes a longer period of time in Scripture, not demanding simultaneity
Acts 24:15: Paul speaks of 'a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and the unjust' — the anarthrous (article-less) form indicates quality, not a single simultaneous event
1 Corinthians 15:23-24: 'each in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, afterward those who are Christ's at His coming. Then comes the end' — a clear sequential order

outline.title: The Nature of the Resurrected Body
subpoints: The bodies of the unsaved will be raised to dishonor — fit for their wicked hearts, perfectly able to suffer forever without relief or cessation
Those in hell will not repent; they will remain in enmity with God eternally
The bodies of the saved are patterned after the resurrection body of Christ (1 Corinthians 15)
Christ's post-resurrection body could eat fish, be touched, and was described as 'flesh and bone' (Luke 24)
The saved body will be imperishable — incapable of corruption or sin — a state better than Adam's pre-fall condition
Adam was able to sin or not sin; after the fall, humanity could only sin; at the resurrection, believers will be unable to sin

outline.title: Summary and Pastoral Exhortation
subpoints: Historic pre-millennialism leaves fewer Scriptures outside the systematic 'bucket' than other Reformed positions
The parable-of-the-kingdom passages (the mustard seed growing to fill the earth) remain difficult for this position
The amillennial position, in the pastor's judgment, accounts for the fewest scriptural data
Every question of eschatological detail is secondary to the primary gospel question: one's relationship to Christ now
Christ is coming back; those in Christ will be raised to eternal life; those outside Christ face eternal condemnation

summary:
In this twelfth installment of the Eschatology series, Pastor Lyro turns to the central passage for understanding the two resurrections: Revelation 20:4-5. He begins by reviewing the structure of Revelation 19-21, arguing that chapters 20 and 21 serve as a detailed commentary on the grand vision of chapter 19, with the whole operating in a recapitulatory rather than strictly sequential manner — similar to the relationship between Genesis 1 and Genesis 2.

The interpretive crux of the lesson is the Greek verb translated 'came to life' (or 'lived') in Revelation 20:4-5. This verb appears twice in rapid succession, just 13 words apart in the original. Most amillennial and postmillennial interpreters understand the first occurrence as a spiritual resurrection (regeneration) and the second as a physical one. Pastor Lyro challenges this reading on grammatical and contextual grounds: the verb is identical in form in both instances, and throughout the entirety of the book of Revelation the same root word consistently denotes physical resurrection (as in Rev. 2:8 and 13:14). To make an exception in verse 4 without any contextual signal requires special pleading.

Reaching beyond Revelation, Pastor Lyro surveys John 5:28-29, Acts 24:15, and 1 Corinthians 15:23-24 to demonstrate that Scripture consistently presents two distinct resurrections — one for the righteous and one for the unjust — separated by an interval of time rather than occurring simultaneously. He engages the grammar of Acts 24:15 at length, showing that Paul's anarthrous (article-less) use of 'just' and 'unjust' describes qualities, not a single simultaneous event, and includes an untranslated 'also' that separates the two groups.

The lesson concludes with a discussion of the nature of the resurrected body: the bodies of the condemned will be suited to eternal suffering without relief; the bodies of the redeemed will be patterned after Christ's glorious body — physical, touchable, capable of eating, and yet imperishable and incapable of sin. The lesson ends with a pastoral call: all of these doctrines are of secondary importance compared to the foundational question of one's present faith in Jesus Christ.
transcript:
## Introduction and Review

The rest of us, if you grab your Bible and turn to Revelation chapter 19 and 20, Revelation 19 and 20. We're approaching the end of our series on Eschatology — and there's no pun intended that we're approaching the end. Today and next Sunday, Lord willing, will be the two last lessons that we have on this series. Last Sunday will be to catch whatever we haven't covered yet. So it shouldn't be too much there. We'll start something new the following Sunday.

Of all the arguments for covenantal pre-millennialism, for historic pre-millennialism, the biblical evidence, as I see it, for two resurrections, is the most convincing to me. Which is interesting because if you're not a historic pre-millennialist — if you're amillennial or postmillennial — often the most convincing argument to you is also the only existence of one resurrection. So we both are in the same boat there.

So we'll try to finish talking about the resurrection today and then conclude the series next Sunday. We started last week looking at our confession. This is the Bible Presbyterian Church version of the Westminster Confession of Faith. If you want to see the unamended version, you can open in your hymnal to page 867 for the original text. But our confession of the Bible Presbyterian Church says this: "At the return of the Lord Jesus Christ, such living persons as are found in Him, shall not die but be changed and all the dead in Christ shall be raised up. With the self-same bodies and none other, although with different qualities, which shall be united again to their souls forever. The bodies of the unjust shall after Christ has reigned on earth a thousand years, be raised by the power of God to dishonor."

So this amendment, in my understanding and belief, brings the confession into alignment with what the Bible teaches — the idea that there would be two separate resurrections of the just and of the unjust.

## Revelation 19 and 20: Structure of John's Vision

I want to start by reviewing what we saw last week about Revelation 19 and Revelation 20 and how they work together. If you look at Revelation 19, verses 11 through 19, you see that passage is speaking of Christ's physical return to the world. Look at verses 11: "Now I saw heaven opened and behold a white horse, and He who sat on him was called Faithful and True. And in righteousness He judges and makes war, His eyes were like a flame of fire and on His head were many crowns. He had a name written that no one knew except Himself. He was clothed with a robe dipped in blood, and His name is called the Word of God..." [continues through verse 19].

What we read here, as I believe, is the second coming of the Lord Jesus Christ, and more than that — it is all the events surrounding the consummation of time. The emphasis of this vision is on the coming of Christ as the conqueror. He is pictured as riding a white horse like a warrior with his army. He will smite the nations with his word.

Now some people say — some amillennialists and some postmillennialists — that this is not a description of the second coming of Christ, but is the operation of the church throughout the ages, happening right now as the gospel goes out and people are converted. If that's the case, if this passage is not speaking about the physical return of Christ, then the book of Revelation does not speak of it at all.

I think the best way to understand this is that Revelation 20 and 21, following Revelation 19, are a commentary on what chapter 19 teaches. John sees his vision in Revelation 19, and then he spends chapters 20 and 21 explaining that vision. It's like Genesis 1 is a description of the whole of creation, and then Genesis 2 is a description of a particular thing — the creation of humanity. So chapters 19, 20, 21 are not sequential, but concurrent. Chapter 19 happens and chapters 20 and 21 describe what chapter 19 is talking about.

## The Crux: Revelation 20:4-5

The crux of the matter for this lesson is Revelation 20, verses 4 and 5. "And I saw thrones and they sat on them, and judgment was committed to them. Then I saw the souls of those who had been beheaded for their witness to Jesus and for the Word of God, who had not worshiped the beast or his image, and did not receive his mark on their foreheads or on their hands, and they lived and reigned with Christ for a thousand years. But the rest of the dead did not live again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection."

Let me start by saying this. I have not found any view, including my own, that satisfactorily addresses these two verses in their entirety. There is a struggle with verse 4 in every one of the Reformed views, and every scholar holds that.

If you have a New King James, the end of verse 4 reads, "and they lived and reigned with Christ for a thousand years." It is better to translate this verb as "they came to life." The verb in the New King James is better understood as "they came to life." The ESV translates it that way — "and they came to life" — which is a better understanding of that verb there.

Just to get a little "Greeky" here — it's a third person verb, so it means he, she, it, or they. It is plural, so it's "they." It is active, so there's something that they're doing — coming to life. It is a verb that describes something that happened in the past from the perspective of the speaker. And it is indicative, meaning it's not a potential thing, but a matter of fact.

So the question is: how did they come to life in verse 4? Spiritual or physical life? Is verse 4 talking about spiritual life or physical life? Verse 5 is universally agreed upon as a physical resurrection. The debate is on the first resurrection in verse 4.

## 'Came to Life' in Scripture: Spiritual or Physical?

In Ephesians chapter 2, verses 1 through 6, a related word is used to indicate our spiritual resurrection — the moment we come to salvation. "But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love with which He loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ." This is clearly a spiritual resurrection.

In John 5:25, the very same word used in Revelation is used to teach a spiritual resurrection: "Most assuredly, I say to you, the hour is coming, and now is, when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God, and those who hear will live." In context, this is talking about a coming to life of faith, not of the physical resurrection.

So the argument is made that because Revelation 20 uses this word, it should be interpreted as spiritual, following these passages. However, there are important differences. In the Gospel of John, the context itself demands a spiritual resurrection. In Revelation 20, the context doesn't demand that. The saints who came to life in Revelation 20 are already redeemed — these are the ones who died for the sake of Christ. So it's not a matter of redemption; it's something that follows redemption.

## How Is This Word Used in Revelation?

How is this word used in the book of Revelation? Turn to Revelation 2:8: "The words of the first and the last, who died and came to life." Who is that talking about? Christ. What is the reference to "who died and came to life"? His death and physical resurrection. That "came to life" is the very same word in Revelation 20.

Revelation 13:14: "telling those who dwell on earth to make an image to the beast who was wounded by the sword and lived." In verse 3 of that same chapter, the beast died. And in verse 14 it says the beast "lived" — again the word "came to life" — and in context it seems to be a literal coming back to life.

And then in Revelation 20:5, as I said, there is agreement that "came to life" there is a physical coming to life. So you see that in the entirety of the book of Revelation, every time this verb is used, it indicates a physical resurrection. The only exception — if that is an exception — would be verse 4. That's not good exegesis to look at how something is used everywhere and then say, "but in the place I want, that's an exception," without any warrant to do that.

Also note: the word "resurrection" (anastasis) is used 42 times in the New Testament. I checked all 42 of them — translated and checked. Every one of them, all 41 in the rest of the Testament, is a literal physical bodily resurrection. This would be the only place where the word resurrection is used in a non-literal way. And again, you look at the context — other than being prophetic apocalyptic literature — you don't get anything from the context that says this is going to be the exception.

## Two Resurrections in Other Scriptures

This interpretation is consistent with the rest of the New Testament. John 5:28-29 says: "Do not marvel at this, for the hour is coming, in which all who are in the graves will hear His voice and come forth — those who have done good, to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil, to the resurrection of condemnation."

Remember first 25, where it says the hour "is coming and now is" — here he says just "the hour is coming," without the "now is." That is going to be a resurrection. And John separates the two resurrections by repeating the phrase "the resurrection of" — unnecessary if they are one event at the same time. Jesus here simply states that there would be a different resurrection for the just and for the unjust, without saying anything about the timing.

Acts 24:15: Paul says, "I have hope in God, which they themselves also accept, that there will be a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and the unjust." Now notice: in the New King James, words added to help understanding are in italics. The words "the" before "dead," "the just," and "the unjust" are all in italics — meaning the article is not there in the original language. This is what's called the anarthrous usage, without the article — indicating not a specific event, but a quality. He's saying there will be a resurrection of those that have the quality of being dead, and among those dead, some that have the quality of being just and some the quality of being unjust. It's not specifying that these things are happening at the same time.

1 Corinthians 15:23-24: "But each one in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, afterward those who are Christ's at His coming. Then comes the end, when He delivers the kingdom to God the Father, when He puts an end to all rule and all authority and power." Paul makes a clear separation: Christ first, then those who are in Christ, then after that comes the end. And verse 26: the end is when death itself is destroyed — consistent with how Revelation chapter 20 goes.

## The Nature of the Resurrected Body

One last thing I want to talk about is the nature of the resurrected body.

The bodies of the unsaved will be raised to dishonor. So they'll have physical substance, but the Scriptures never speak of the body of the unsaved when they're raised as a glorious body. These bodies — they'll not be glorious, they'll fit their wicked hearts. And they will be bodies perfectly able to suffer forever. If you're being burnt, eventually that's going to stop hurting because you'll die. That's not how suffering forever goes. Bodies perfectly able to suffer forever without ever stopping or relief. And all the while, cursing God — there's not going to be anybody in eternal hell who desires to go to heaven. They're still going to be in enmity with God even then.

The bodies of the saved will be the same bodies as during life, but with a physical substance patterned after the body of Christ. 1 Corinthians 15 says they're going to be patterned after the body of Christ. Christ was able to eat fish. He was able to be touched — "put your hand on my side." His body is called "flesh and bone" in Luke 24. It is incorruptible — it cannot be changed or corrupted.

Adam was created in a state of being able to sin and able not to sin. After the fall, humanity was put in a state where they could only sin. When we're redeemed, we're put back in a state similar to where Adam was — able not to sin (as evidenced by Romans 6). But at the resurrection, we're going to be in a state of not being able to sin — better than what Adam was. Incorruptible.

## Conclusion

At the end of the day, this is what matters: Christ is going back. His people will be forever with him in a bodily form, and unbelievers will keep on dying forever — that's how hell is described, the state of dying forever. And that is decided right now, not in the future, but right now. What you believe concerning Christ is what decides what kind of coming you are going to experience. Are you going to be those that Christ is going to destroy at his coming, or those that he's going to raise to life eternal with him? That's really where the important stuff is.

*[Questions and dismissal prayer]*
